coredata.dc:title: Synergistic lanthanum /nitrogen co-modified carbon dots as steel corrosion inhibitor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Haitao
Wei, Can
Ye, Yuwei
Wang, Zhixiang
Zhou, Shengguo
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have significantly contributed to corrosion resistance due to their varied physical and chemical properties, excellent biocompatibility, and high surface activity. Therefore, designing and developing high-performance carbon dot inhibitors is of great significance. The study successfully synthesized lanthanum and nitrogen co-modified carbon dots (La/N-CDs), which function as a green and effective corrosion inhibitor. It was found that the La/N-CDs inhibitor presented a nanostructure with size of 3–9 nm. Electrochemical experiments, such as Tafel plots and EIS, showed that the presence of La/N-CDs effectively suppressed metal corrosion in a chloride-containing corrosion solution. In a 3 wt% NaCl solution, 400 mg/L La15 %/N-CDs exhibited the best corrosion inhibition performance, with an impedance modulus of 2165.33 Ω cm² and an inhibition efficiency reaching as high as 93.07 %, while the surface roughness of the steel and the corrosion rate was also minimized. This might be explained by the formation of an adsorption layer by La/N-CDs on the metal surface, a phenomenon confirmed by Langmuir isotherm and XPS investigations. These findings confirmed that La/N-CDs could form a protective film on the metal surface, significantly improving its corrosion resistance and extending its service life in corrosion environment. The study’s findings offered insights and a conceptual structure for enhancing corrosion inhibitors using carbon dots that were augmented with rare earth elements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rare earth
Carbon dots
Corrosion inhibitor
Adsorption film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images at different resolutions (a-c) and particle size distribution (d) of La/N-CDs.
FTIR (a) and UV–vis (b) of La/N-CDs with different La contents.
High-resolution XPS spectra of La0 %/N-CDs (a-c) and La20 %/N-CDs (d-g), and XPS full spectrum (h).
EIS of Q235 steel immersed for 24 h in different La/N-CDs solutions: La0 %/N-CDs (a1-a3), La5 %/N-CDs (b1-b3), La15 %/N-CDs (c1-c3), and La20 %/N-CDs (d1-d3).
EIS equivalent circuit diagrams without (a) and with (b) CDs.
Tafel plots of Q235 steel in different solutions: La0 %/N-CDs (a), La5 %/N-CDs (b), La15 %/N-CDs (c), and La20 %/N-CDs (d).
Corrosion rate of steel at different La/N-CDs concentrations (a) and different immersion times (b).
Steel surface morphology after immersion in different solutions: Blank (a), 400 mg/L of La0 %/N-CDs (b), La5 %/N-CDs (c), La15 %/N-CDs (d), and La20 %/N-CDs (e).
SEM corrosion morphology analysis of Q235 after immersion in different solutions: Blank (a), La0 %/N-CDs (b), La5 %/N-CDs (c), La15 %/N-CDs (d), and La20 %/N-CDs (e).
XPS spectra of the steel surface after 24 h immersion in La15 %/N-CDs solution: XPS full spectrum (a), N 1 s (b), and La 3d (c).
Langmuir adsorption isotherms of steel immersed in different solutions: La0 %/N-CDs (a), La5 %/N-CDs (b), La15 %/N-CDs (c) and La20 %/N-CDs (d).
EIS fitting parameters for different concentrations of La/N-CDs.
Corrosion parameters of Q235 steel in 3 wt% NaCl solution with varying concentrations of La/N-CDs.
Summary of the latest corrosion inhibition performance of carbon dots.

row.entry: Citric acid monohydrate, N, N'-dicyclohexyl-carbodiimide, 4-dimethylaminopyridine, melamine, ethanol, triethylamine
Lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate, citric acid anhydrous

head.ce:title: Synergistic lanthanum /nitrogen co-modified carbon dots as steel corrosion inhibitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have significantly contributed to corrosion resistance due to their varied physical and chemical properties, excellent biocompatibility, and high surface activity. Therefore, designing and developing high-performance carbon dot inhibitors is of great significance. The study successfully synthesized lanthanum and nitrogen co-modified carbon dots (La/N-CDs), which function as a green and effective corrosion inhibitor. It was found that the La/N-CDs inhibitor presented a nanostructure with size of 3–9 nm. Electrochemical experiments, such as Tafel plots and EIS, showed that the presence of La/N-CDs effectively suppressed metal corrosion in a chloride-containing corrosion solution. In a 3 wt% NaCl solution, 400 mg/L La15 %/N-CDs exhibited the best corrosion inhibition performance, with an impedance modulus of 2165.33 Ω cm² and an inhibition efficiency reaching as high as 93.07 %, while the surface roughness of the steel and the corrosion rate was also minimized. This might be explained by the formation of an adsorption layer by La/N-CDs on the metal surface, a phenomenon confirmed by Langmuir isotherm and XPS investigations. These findings confirmed that La/N-CDs could form a protective film on the metal surface, significantly improving its corrosion resistance and extending its service life in corrosion environment. The study’s findings offered insights and a conceptual structure for enhancing corrosion inhibitors using carbon dots that were augmented with rare earth elements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the energy crisis has been intensifying. The ocean contains abundant resources and energy such as oil and gas fields, and if effectively utilized, it will greatly improve living standards. Therefore, the development of the ocean is urgent [1,2]. However, during the implementation process, metal equipment cannot avoid contact with seawater and is prone to severe corrosion, leading to premature failure [3–5]. In order to alleviate corrosion, the most commonly used method in existing oil fields is to use corrosion inhibitors, which mainly divided into organic (such as phosphines, amines) and inorganic (such as chromates, phosphates) corrosion inhibitors [6–9]. Organic inhibitors can adsorb on metal surface to form an anti-corrosion barrier, while inorganic inhibitors can form a protective layer to protect the metal [10–12]. These inhibitors are essential for improving the development of oil and gas fields in the ocean. Nevertheless, their individual application has limitations in terms of effectiveness, thus developing new corrosion inhibitors is imperative [13–16].

Carbon Dots (CDs), as a new type of nanomaterial, have gradually emerged in anti-corrosion fields due to their excellent chemical stability, environmental friendliness, and good dispersibility [17]. CDs can not only significantly enhance the anti-corrosion performance of metal but also reduce the emission of harmful substances, making them a research hotspot and development direction in the anti-corrosion field [18]. Ye et al. [19] synthesized N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by using citric acid and histidine in an oil bath method. The inhibition rate of N-CDs as a corrosion inhibitor in a 0.1 mol/L HCl environment exceeded 90 %, achieving protection for Q235 steel. Cui et al. [20] prepared N-CDs using 4-aminosalicylic acid as a raw material. The inhibitory impact on carbon steel increased as the concentration of CDs and immersion duration increased. The improvement occurred as a result of the creation of a protective film on the carbon steel surface by CDs, effectively isolating the corrosive substance from the steel. Kalajahi et al. [21] prepared metal ion-doped carbon dots using CuCl2·H2O as a dopant. The results indicated that Cu-CDs created a protective layer on the X60 steel surface, enhancing its resistance to charge transfer and inhibiting corrosion. Additionally, the antibacterial properties of the steel were enhanced.

The non-metallic element nitrogen and the rare earth element lanthanum were chosen as doping components for this investigation due to their exceptional corrosion prevention properties and environmentally friendly characteristics. A novel inhibitor, La/N-CDs, was prepared by modifying carbon dots. The synergistic protective behavior and mechanisms of lanthanum, nitrogen, and carbon dots in corrosion inhibition were analyzed in depth. The composition and structure of the as-prepared CDs were analyzed using TEM, FTIR, UV–vis, and XPS. The corrosion inhibition performance of the as-prepared CDs was analyzed using EIS and Tafel plots. SEM and SPM were used to obtain the corrosion morphology of Q235 steel after corrosion tests. XPS and Langmuir adsorption isotherms were used to investigate the adsorption mechanism of the prepared CDs.

Citric acid anhydrous (AR, 99.5 %) was obtained from Shandong Xiya Chemical Co., Ltd. Lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate (AR, 99 %) was provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine monohydrate (AR, 98 %) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The testing electrode was selected as the Q235 steel with dimensions of 1×1 cm². After being cleaned using ultrasonic equipment in absolute ethanol, it was polished using sandpaper with grits of 400, 800, and 1200.

Fig. 1 depicted a schematic diagram that illustrated the process of preparing CDs. Initially, various proportions of lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate (0 %, 5 %, 15 %, 20 %) and citric acid anhydrous were measured using a precise electronic balance. Subsequently, the measured substances were combined and dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water. After that, 1 mL of ethylenediamine was added to the solution, followed by sonication of the mixture for a duration of 15 min. The sonicated mixture was placed into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h in an oven. To improve the purity of CDs, the obtained reaction product was filtered with a 0.22 µm membrane and dialyzed with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. Finally, the filtered solution was freeze-dried to obtain brown powder. The as-prepared CDs were named La/N-CDs, and the carbon dots with varying La additions were named as La0 %/N-CDs, La5 %/N-CDs, La15 %/N-CDs, and La20 %/N-CDs, respectively.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200, Japan) was used to analyze the morphology and microstructure of La/N-CDs at a voltage of 200 kV. Before test, a certain concentration of La/N-CDs solution was dripped onto a copper mesh and placed in a vacuum drying oven at 50 °C for drying. The characterization of the La/N-CDs structure was conducted using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Alpha BRUKER, Germany) and UV–Visible spectrometry (UV–Vis, UV–2550, Japan). A small amount of La/N-CDs powder was mixed with KBr powder and ground for FTIR testing. A low concentration of La/N-CDs solution was used for UV–vis testing. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD, USA) was used to examine the composition of La/N-CDs.

The experimental setup included four distinct La/N-CDs, dissolved in a 3 wt% NaCl solution at various concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 400 mg/L). A CHI660E workstation with a three-electrode system was used for the corrosion tests. The sample served as the working electrode, a 1×1 cm2 platinum sheet was used as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode was used as the reference electrode. At a scan rate of 1 mV/s, Tafel curves were produced by changing the voltage around the open circuit potential (OCP) within a range of ±450 mV. With a voltage amplitude of 5 mV, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted throughout a frequency range of 105 to 10−2 Hz. All immersion experiments and electrochemical tests were conducted in a constant temperature environment at 298 K.

The corrosion rate was determined by weight loss analysis. All Q235 steel samples used for testing were cleaned ultrasonically with alcohol three times, each session lasting 30 min. The steel samples, sized 1×1×1 cm, were polished with 400, 800, 1200, and 1500 grit sandpaper, then cleaned with alcohol and stored in a dry environment. The dry weight of the steel blocks was measured at a constant temperature of 298 K, which served as the initial weight. The samples were immersed in a 3 wt% NaCl solution with varying concentrations of La/N-CDs, with an exposed area of 1 cm². After immersion, the blocks were thoroughly rinsed with distilled water, ultrasonically cleaned with alcohol, and reweighed after drying. The corrosion rate was calculated using Eq. 1:
(1)
v
corr
=
K ∙ W
A ∙ T ∙ D
Where
v
corr
was the corrosion rate, K was a constant with a value of 8.76×104, W was the weight loss, A was the exposed surface area in the solution, T was the immersion time, and D was the density of Q235 steel [22].

Following the electrochemical testing, the Q235 steel samples were thoroughly cleaned using ethanol and distilled water. The corrosion morphology was examined using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Quanta FEG 250, USA) with an electron gun acceleration voltage of 15 kV. Surface roughness analysis was performed using a scanning probe microscope (SPM, Dimension ICON, USA). The area of the roughness test scanning region was 5×5 µm2. The composition of the adsorption film was analyzed using XPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, FTIR and UV–vis of La/N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The distribution of the as-prepared lanthanum and nitrogen co-modified La/N-CDs was analyzed using TEM. The results showed that a large number of nanoparticles dispersed between 6‐8 nm as shown in Fig. 2a-c. Most of the La/N-CDs particles were spherical, with high dispersion and uniform size distribution, indicating good dispersion in deionized water. By measured, the average diameter of the La/N-CDs was found in Fig. 2d to be 7.06±1.29 nm. The HRTEM image in Fig. 2c showed well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 2.08 Å for the La/N-CDs.

Fig. 3 showed the FTIR and UV–vis spectra of La/N-CDs with varying La contents. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 3a revealed a sequence of absorption peaks in the La/N-CDs. The characteristic peak at 3446–3433 cm-¹ corresponded to –N–H and –O–H bonds, indicating the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups in the product [23]. The symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of –C–H were identified as the cause of the faint characteristic peaks at 2936–2930 cm−1, suggesting the existence of –CH₂ and –CH₃ groups in the product [24]. The peaks found at 1696, 1586, and 1439 cm⁻¹ corresponded to the stretching vibration of the carbonyl group (–CO), the bending vibration of the amine group (–N–H), and the stretching vibration of the methyl group (–C–H), respectively. These findings indicated successful doping of nitrogen elements, forming stable chemical bonds with adjacent carbon atoms, and confirming the effective reaction between the amino groups of ethylenediamine and the carboxyl groups of anhydrous citric acid [25]. The peak at 1042 cm-¹ represented the La–OH bond, while the strong absorption peak at 1394 cm-¹ was attributed to the stretching vibration of the anionic NO3 - in the residual La(NO3)3·6 H2O raw material, which significantly decreased in La0 %/N-CDs [26]. The peak at 1160–1158 cm-¹ corresponded to the stretching vibration of the –C–O bond, and the peak at 755–751 cm-¹ corresponded to the La–H bond, indicating successful doping of lanthanum elements [27]. From the UV–vis spectra in Fig. 3b, an absorption peak could be observed at 338 nm, which might be related to the n-π* electronic transition of the –CN bond. The absorption peak exhibited a redshift as the La content increased, where the absorption peak shifted from 338 to 341 nm as the La doping concentration increased. The redshift indicated a decrease in the bandgap of La/N-CDs, possibly due to the electronic structure changes induced by La doping. The redshift also suggested an enhanced interaction between the doped elements and the carbon dots, which might lead to changes in the local electronic environment.

To determine the composition of the as-prepared La/N-CDs, both high-resolution spectrum analyses as shown in Fig. 4a-g and full spectra in Fig. 4h were conducted. The C 1 s XPS spectrum of La0 %/N-CDs revealed in Fig. 4a displayed four distinct peaks at 284.28, 284.88, 285.81, and 288.08 eV. These peaks corresponded to the presence of CC, CN, CO, and OCN bonds, respectively. A C–H peak was seen in Fig. 4d at 284.18 eV following the addition of lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate [28]. Deconvolution of the N 1 s XPS spectrum of La0 %/N-CDs in Fig. 4b revealed the presence of three distinct peaks: 398.65 eV (CNC), 398.93 eV (CN), and 400.11 eV (CNH) [29]. Three peaks were visible at 398.39 eV (Pyridinic N), 399.15 eV (Pyrrolic N), and 399.99 eV (Graphitic N) in the N 1 s spectrum of La20 %/N-CDs as shown in Fig. 4e [30]. The O 1 s XPS spectrum of La0 %/N-CDs in Fig. 4c could be separated into distinct peaks at 529.90 eV (CO), 530.38 eV (OCO), and 531.40 eV (COH). These peaks in Fig. 4f indicated the presence of functional groups that were formed during the synthesis of carbon dots and the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups from anhydrous citric acid [31]. The La 3d spectrum in Fig. 4g could be explained as follows: The XPS spectrum of La 3d5/2 exhibited two distinct peaks at 833.61 eV and 838.13 eV, which suggested that La was present in the +3-oxidation state in La/N-CDs [27]. The peaks of La 3d3/2 were separated into several components, with a main peak detected at 853.82 eV and additional satellite peaks identified at 864.52 eV. With the introduction of La(NO3)3·6 H2O, the binding energies of CN (284.95 eV), CO (285.92 eV), and OC (531.27 eV) shifted to higher values. This was due to the reduced outer electron cloud density and weakened inner electron shielding effect, which increased the Coulomb force between electrons and the atomic nucleus, thus increasing the binding energy. This indicated the formation of coordination bonds between the functional groups and La, confirming the successful preparation of La/N-CDs.

The effectiveness and underlying mechanism of the prepared La/N-CDs will be examined by discussing their inhibition performance. Fig. 5 showed the EIS diagrams of all Q235 steel samples immersed for 24 h with different doping amounts and concentrations of carbon dot inhibitors. From the Nyquist plots as shown in Fig. 5a1-d1, the observation revealed that the radius of the capacitive arc exhibited an increase upon the introduction of La/N-CDs. This suggested that the La/N-CDs synthesized were capable of creating a highly efficient film on the surface of the electrode [32]. The radius of the capacitive arc exhibited a positive correlation with the inhibitor concentration, which might be attributed to the enhanced adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the steel surface [33]. Put simply, the density and corrosion resistance of the protective coating were enhanced as the inhibitor concentration increased. The radius of the capacitive arc rose proportionally with the rising La doping concentration. However, with further addition of La, the impedance capacitive arc radius of La20 %/N-CDs decreased compared to La15 %/N-CDs, suggesting that excessive addition of La reduced the corrosion resistance of CDs. This indicated that using an ideal quantity of the rare earth element La might significantly enhance the corrosion inhibition capability of the produced CDs. However, excessive addition of La could actually accelerate the corrosion of the electrode.

By analyzing the impedance modules in Fig. 5a2-d2 and phase angle in Fig. 5a3-d3, it was noted that the impedance modulus at the lowest frequency exhibited an inverse relationship with the corrosion rate of the electrode [34]. At the same La doping level, the |Z|0.01 Hz value increased with the concentration of carbon dots. In Fig. 5a2-c2, |Z|0.01 Hz showed an increasing trend with the increase in La content, while in Fig. 5d2, |Z|0.01 Hz decreased in La20 %/N-CDs. The |Z|0.01 Hz values of 400 mg/L solutions of La0 %/N-CDs, La5 %/N-CDs, La15 %/N-CDs, and La20 %/N-CDs reached 1547.41, 2127.61, 2165.33, and 1788.34 Ω cm², respectively, after 24 h of immersion. Since the impedance modulus |Z|0.01 Hz of La15 %/N-CDs was the highest, it could be inferred that La15 %/N-CDs exhibited the best corrosion inhibition performance. Additionally, during the corrosion tests, the phase angle curves of different CDs inhibitors presented an upward trend, with peak heights increasing with concentration, indicating reduced corrosion on the steel surface [35].

The analogous circuit diagram of the EIS was shown in Fig. 6, which offered a more thorough explanation of the corrosion kinetics. The solution resistance (R
s ), film resistance (R
f ), charge transfer resistance (R
ct ), film capacitance (Qf), and double layer capacitance (Qdl) were all shown in this image [36]. The parameters obtained from fitting calculations were shown in Table 1. The R
s values for all test solutions were stable and similar during the tests, while other parameters displayed significant changes. Furthermore, the correlation between the value of R
f and the concentration of La/N-CDs suggested the creation of a highly efficient adsorption film on the surface of the steel. Overall, R
ct exhibited a positive correlation with the rising concentration of carbon dots in the solution. This occurred because the water molecules on the surface of the steel were substituted by the La/N-CDs. As a result, the steel's contact area with the corrosive medium decreased, leading to a deceleration in the corrosion process [37].

The surface coverage (θ) and corrosion inhibition efficiency (IE) could be determined by utilizing Eq. 2 and Eq. 3:
(2)
θ =
i
corr
0
−
i
corr
i
corr
0
(3)
IE =
i
corr
0
−
i
corr
i
corr
0
× 100 %
Here,
i
corr
0
denoted the corrosion current density in the solution without inhibitors, while
i
corr
represented the corrosion current density in solutions with varying concentrations of CDs [39]. Based on the data presented in Fig. 7, it was evident that
i
corr
of the steel was significantly elevated in the solution lacking CDs inhibitors, indicating a substantial corrosion rate. The introduction of CDs resulted in a substantial alteration of the corrosion current density. The corrosion current density in CDs solutions with low concentration exhibited a decrease, although the extent of reduction was restricted. The corrosion current density in CDs solutions of medium concentration decreased, indicating a notable enhancement in corrosion resistance. The corrosion current density in high concentration CDs solutions decreased significantly, by a factor of ten, indicating a substantial improvement in the corrosion resistance of steel in the corrosive environment containing CDs. Furthermore, when the concentrations of CDs increased, the level of corrosion resistance also increased progressively.

Table 2 demonstrated a clear correlation between the addition of CDs and the increasing trend of the R
p value. The R
p value gradually rose in accordance with the concentration of CDs. These results demonstrated that the CDs efficiently adsorbed onto the steel, creating a protective film that covered the surface. This film reduced the interaction between the steel and the corrosive medium, consequently decelerating the corrosion process. The calculation of the inhibition efficiency (η) could be determined using Eq. 4:
(4)
η =
R
p
− R
p , 0
R
p
× 100 %
Here, R
p and R
p,0 represented the polarization resistance in the solution with and without inhibitors, respectively [40]. The η value effectively evaluated the inhibition efficiency. The calculated η value increased with the concentration change of CDs. When the CDs concentration increased to 400 mg/L, η reached 80.10 % for La0 %/N-CDs, 90.74 % for La5 %/N-CDs, 93.07 % for La15 %/N-CDs, but dropped to 77.15 % for La20 %/N-CDs. The findings demonstrated that the introduction of rare earth element La in suitable quantities enhanced the ability of corrosion inhibitors to protect Q235 steel in a 3 wt% NaCl solution, effectively preventing corrosion. Conversely, excessive addition of La could affect the corrosion inhibition effect of CDs in the corrosive medium, accelerating the corrosion of steel by the corrosive medium.
Additionally, a comparison of the corrosion inhibition performance between the rare-earth-doped N-CDs prepared in this study and recent carbon dot research was presented, as shown in Table 3.

The steel was immersed in 3 wt% NaCl solutions containing different inhibitors, and the test results were presented in Fig. 8. As shown in Fig. 8a, the corrosion rate of the steel decreased rapidly with the addition of inhibitors. As the inhibitor concentration increased, the rate of decline in corrosion slowed but remained overall downward. La/N-CDs exhibited excellent corrosion inhibition performance, significantly reducing the corrosion of steel in a neutral Cl⁻-containing environment. In Fig. 8b, the analysis of immersion time and corrosion rate is also provided, demonstrating that as immersion time increased, the corrosion rate gradually declined, and the weight loss of the steel further slowed. The weight loss analysis revealed that the corrosion rate of La15 %/N-CDs was the lowest, showing the best corrosion inhibition performance, consistent with the electrochemical test results.

The three-dimensional structure of Q235 steel in different solutions was shown in Fig. 9. Evidently, the steel displayed distinct properties at varying inhibitor concentrations. After immersion in a 3 wt% NaCl solution for 24 h, extensive protrusions were observed on the surface of the steel as shown in Fig. 9a due to the strong attack of corrosive ions, with a surface roughness of 111.5±8.5 nm. The introduction of as-prepared CDs resulted in a significant reduction in both the protruded areas and surface roughness as shown in Fig. 9b-e. The steel’s surface roughness, after being submerged for 24 h in a 400 mg/L solution of different CDs in a 3 wt% NaCl solution, measured 47.44±11.3, 44.8±4.0, 23.9±2.9, and 26.9±2.1 nm, respectively. In comparison to the control group, the surface roughness of the steel decreased by 57.49 % in the La0 %/N-CDs solution, indicating that the introduction of CDs effectively mitigated steel corrosion. With the increase in La content, the surface roughness of the steel further decreased by 59.82 %, 78.57 %, and 75.87 %. Out of all the samples, La15 %/ N-CDs demonstrated the most effective inhibition effect, which was in line with the corrosion morphology.

Fig. 10 illustrated the several forms of corrosion that occurred on Q235 steel when it was immersed, providing a visual representation of the effects of SEM corrosion. In Fig. 10a, after being immersed for 24 h, the steel surface exhibited noticeable pits when exposed to the corrosive solution, indicating ongoing corrosion. However, after the addition of CDs, a thin film was observed covering the steel surface. Compared to the control group, relatively smoother surfaces were found in Fig. 10b-c, with the steel surface covered by La15 %/N-CDs being the smoothest as shown in Fig. 10d, indicating the least corrosion. In comparison to the La0 %/N-CDs solution with a concentration of 400 mg/L, the steel surface treated with the La5 %/N-CDs solution at the same concentration exhibited a relatively smoother texture. With the increase in La content, the surface became even flatter, providing further evidence of the inhibitory effect of the as-prepared CDs. The elemental analysis revealed a uniform distribution of Fe, C, N, and La across the surface of the steel. The presence of an adsorption film on the steel surface was verified by the detection of Fe from the Q235 steel and the presence of C, N, and La components from the as-prepared CDs.

Fig. 11 displayed the XPS analyses of the steel surface conducted during corrosion testing. This analysis was used to determine the kind of adsorption that CDs had on the steel surface. In Fig. 11a XPS investigation revealed the existence of La and N elements after the immersion of the steel substrate in La/N-CDs solution. This suggested that the corrosion inhibitor obtained from La/N-CDs was successfully bonded to the steel surface. The N 1 s XPS spectrum was well matched in Fig. 11b using Gaussian fitting, resulting in three peaks at 397.38, 398.54, and 400.45 eV. The first peak was ascribed to the existence of non-protonated nitrogen atoms (N–C) in the CDs inhibitor. The second peak demonstrated the coordinated nitrogen (N–Fe) interaction between the CDs inhibitor and the steel surface. The third peak corresponded to the process of protonating the nitrogen in the CDs inhibitor, which was accomplished by introducing a hydrogen atom [47]. In the XPS results of La 3d shown in Fig. 11c, it could be observed that the binding energy of La 3d5/2 shifted from 833.61 eV to 833.48 eV, and the satellite peak shifted from 838.13 eV to 836.58 eV, indicating the formation of stable La2O3, which helped to isolate the corrosive medium. Hence, the presence of nitrogen on the steel surface suggested that the corrosion inhibitor has been adsorbed by both chemical and physical mechanisms, aligning with findings from previous research [48].

The calculation of adsorption isotherms might be performed using the Langmuir isotherm adsorption model, as described by Eq. 5:
(5)
θ
1 − θ
=
K
ads
C

Here, θ was used to represent the surface coverage, C was used to signify the concentration of La/N-CDs, and
K
ads
represented the adsorption equilibrium constant [49]. The correlation between them was seen in Fig. 12. From the figure, it could be seen that the R² values for La0 %/N-CDs, La5 %/N-CDs, La15 %/N-CDs, and La20 %/N-CDs were 0.9983, 0.9996, 0.996, and 0.9506, respectively, indicating an ideal linear correlation. The results showed that the adsorption process during the corrosion tests followed the Langmuir isotherm adsorption model. In general,
K
ads
could be utilized to assess the inhibitory efficacy of the as-prepared CDs samples. A higher
K
ads
value indicated a more potent adsorption [50]. The calculated
K
ads
values for different La contents were listed in Fig. 12, with the order of magnitude being La20 %/N-CDs < La0 %/N-CDs < La5 %/N-CDs < La15 %/N-CDs. As the La content rose, the
K
ads
also increased, resulting in an improved adsorption capacity of the carbon dots at the interface between the steel and the solution. A dense adsorption film formed on the steel surface, effectively slowing down the corrosion by the corrosive medium. Out of all the inhibitors tested, La15 %/N-CDs inhibitors exhibited the greatest
K
ads
, the most powerful adsorption capacity, and the most effective protective effect, which aligned with the findings obtained from EIS analysis.

Moreover, the Gibbs free energy of adsorption (
∆
G
ads
0
) could be determined by employing Eq. 6:
(6)
∆
G
ads
0
= − RT ln ( 1000
K
ads
)

In this context, R was the symbol for the universal gas constant, which had a value of 8.314 J mol−1 K−1. Similarly, T represented the absolute temperature, which was 298 K.
∆
G
ads
0
could be used to distinguish the type of adsorption [47]. In the literature, it was widely accepted that if
∆
G
ads
0
was −20 kJ/mol or larger, the adsorption was categorized as physical adsorption. Conversely, if
∆
G
ads
0
was less than or equal to −40 kJ/mol, the adsorption was classified as chemical adsorption [51]. The average
∆
G
ads
0
values calculated for the four kinds of carbon dots at different concentrations were listed in Fig. 12. The
∆
G
ads
0
values for La0 %/N-CDs, La5 %/N-CDs, La15 %/N-CDs, and La20 %/N-CDs were −25.12, −26.54, −26.05, and −22.13 kJ/mol, respectively. These values suggested that both physisorption and chemisorption occurred at the interface between the steel and the solution.

From the characterization results provided above, the corrosion protection mechanism of the La/N-CDs inhibitors for Q235 steel in a 3 wt% NaCl solution could be deduced, as illustrated in Fig. 13. Typically, Cl⁻ in NaCl solution disrupted the passive film (Fe2O3) formed on the steel surface, causing localized breakdown of the passive film, creating active regions, and increasing the corrosion rate. Cl⁻ could penetrate the passive film and combined with Fe²+ ions to form FeCl2, a soluble iron chloride would further dissolve, intensifying local corrosion and leading to pitting and crevice corrosion. Cl⁻ ions increased the conductivity of the solution, making electrochemical reactions more likely to occur, thus accelerating the overall corrosion process.

The inclusion of La/N-CDs in the 3 wt% NaCl solution facilitated the formation of physical adsorption at the interface between the steel and the solution. Upon prolonged soaking, a thick and impermeable protective film developed on the steel surface. This film acted as both a physical barrier and a deterrent to the infiltration of Cl⁻ ions, thereby minimizing direct contact between the metal surface and the corrosive medium. The functional groups presented on the surface of La/N-CDs established durable N-Fe bonds with the metal surface, enhancing the chemical stability of the surface and consequently minimizing the likelihood of corrosion reactions. Hence, the combined influence of physical and chemical adsorption, facilitated by La/N-CDs, diminished the diffusion and infiltration of the corrosive substance, thereby efficiently averting localized corrosion. Thus, it could be deduced that the existence of La/N-CDs modified the electrochemical characteristics of the metal surface, improved its electrochemical durability, decreased the rate of corrosion current density, and decelerated the corrosion process.

This study systematically investigated the microstructure characterization and corrosion behavior of carbon dots prepared from anhydrous citric acid, ethylenediamine, and lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate in a 3 wt% NaCl solution. XPS analyses indicated that in the as-prepared CDs, La atoms existed in the forms of LaO, LaN, LaO, and La-H bonds, while N atoms existed in the forms of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N. TEM results showed that the La/N-CDs prepared by this method presented a small particle size of 3–9 nm. EIS results indicated that the introduction of La improved the corrosion inhibition performance of CDs, and the inhibition effect became more pronounced with the increasing concentration of CDs. Additionally, Tafel curves showed that the inhibition efficiencies of La0 %/N-CDs, La5 %/N-CDs, La15 %/N-CDs, and La20 %/N-CDs were 80.10 %, 90.74 %, 93.07 %, and 77.15 %, respectively. These results supported the conclusions drawn from the analyses of the EIS data. Among them, La15 %/N-CDs exhibited the best corrosion inhibition performance. This was also confirmed by the weight loss analysis. Corrosion morphological analyses further confirmed the results of the electrochemical analyses. These CDs demonstrated good inhibition capabilities, indicating that the surface of Q235 steel was coated with a compact adsorption coating. The Langmuir adsorption model effectively characterized the adsorption behavior of CDs throughout the corrosion experiments, as shown by the computed values of
K
ads
and
∆
G
ads
0
. The adsorption occurring at the interface between the steel and the solution involved both chemical and physical adsorption, as verified by XPS investigation.

Shengguo Zhou: Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhixiang Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Can Wei: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Yuwei Ye: Methodology, Formal analysis. Haitao Yin: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by funding from the Education Department of Jiangxi Province (Grant No. GJJ2200827), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52461016), and the Key Research and Development Program of Jiangxi Province (20224BBE51041).
